ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers egg dishes broadly; no specific bread omelette code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for bread omelette. General egg dish concepts do not precisely match this composite food.

serving.description: 1 serving (2 slices of bread with omelette)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of bread omelette consists of two slices of white bread encasing or topped with a pan-fried egg omelette, weighing approximately 180 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Egg omelette and white bread composite — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Egg, whole, cooked, fried (NDB 01128) and Bread, white, commercially prepared (NDB 18069)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for bread omelette exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or ICMR-NIN databases. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy data: approximately 55% egg omelette (fried whole egg) and 45% white bread by weight, reflecting a typical 180 g serving of two bread slices (~80 g bread) and one to two eggs (~100 g cooked egg). Nutrient values are per 100 g of the composite food.

gi_source.title: White bread GI reference and egg omelette composite estimate — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bread omelette as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant glycemic driver being white bread (GI ~70–75 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008), moderated downward by the significant egg protein and fat content (~55% of the dish by weight), which slows gastric emptying and reduces the overall glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 42.3) / 100 ≈ 27.6, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 23.5 × 1.8 = 42.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~684 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit bread omelette consumption to occasional servings, as the sodium from commercially prepared white bread and seasoning can contribute meaningfully to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; choosing low-sodium bread and reducing added salt during preparation can lower the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~65) driven by white bread; moderate glycemic load per serving (~27.6)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume bread omelette in controlled portions, as the white bread component raises the glycemic index to the medium range; substituting wholegrain or high-fibre bread can reduce the glycemic response, and the egg protein content provides some buffering of postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol (~210 mg per 100 g from eggs) and saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the cholesterol and saturated fat contributed by eggs in bread omelette; while current evidence suggests dietary cholesterol has a modest effect on LDL in most individuals, those with hypercholesterolaemia should limit egg-based dishes to two to three servings per week in line with clinical dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bread omelette is a popular street food and hawker dish found across Malaysia and Singapore, commonly served at Indian Muslim (Mamak) stalls, kopitiam coffee shops, and casual eateries. The dish is made by beating one or two whole eggs with salt, pepper, and optional aromatics such as finely chopped onion and green chilli, then pan-frying the egg mixture in oil until set. Two slices of white bread are pressed into the egg while it is still soft, or the cooked omelette is folded and sandwiched between the bread slices. The result is a warm, golden, slightly crispy exterior with a soft, eggy interior. The flavour is savoury and mildly rich, with the egg providing a creamy texture that contrasts with the lightly toasted bread. Nutritionally, bread omelette is a moderate-energy food (~218 kcal per 100 g) providing meaningful protein (~9.2 g per 100 g) from eggs and carbohydrate (~23.5 g per 100 g) from white bread. It is a source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, and choline from eggs, and provides iron and B vitamins from the bread. The medium glycemic index (~65) and moderate glycemic load per serving (~27.6) make portion control relevant for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. Bread omelette is vegetarian and typically halal, making it accessible across dietary communities in Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bread omelette?
Is bread omelette suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of bread omelette?
What are the nutrients in bread omelette?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for bread omelette as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy data for fried whole egg and white bread. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, moderated by egg content. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the composite estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a 55:45 weight ratio of cooked egg to white bread using USDA SR Legacy data (NDB 01128 and NDB 18069). Actual restaurant servings may vary in egg count, bread type, oil quantity, and added seasonings, which will affect energy, fat, and sodium values. 2. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g; this is confirmed zero as neither white bread nor fried egg contributes meaningful vitamin C, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for both components. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting trace trans fat from commercial white bread; this is a trace value consistent with USDA SR Legacy data. 4. GI is estimated at 65 (medium) based on white bread as the primary glycemic driver, moderated by egg protein and fat; no direct GI measurement for bread omelette was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite and are not individually reported in a single approved source entry for this dish. 6. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' is preserved from the input record; bread omelette could also be classified under 'Mixed' given the significant bread component, but 'Dairy and eggs' is retained as the egg is the primary defining ingredient. 7. Cuisine is classified as Malaysian per the input record; the dish is also widely served in Singapore and has Indian subcontinental origins, hence multilingual names include Tamil and Hindi as mandatory Singaporean-context languages.
